items.title: Risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors

  • Age ≥50 years is the main non-modifiable factor; 90% of cases occur in >50 years.
  • Tobacco increases the risk of adenomas and colorectal cancer (RR 1.2-1.4), especially in the rectum.
  • Obesity (BMI >30) is associated with a higher incidence of colon cancer, mediated by hyperinsulinemia and IGF-1.
  • Others: type 2 diabetes, alcohol, rich diet in red and processed meats, low fiber intake.

items.title: Screening
items.contentHtml:
Screening

  • Average population: start at 50 years (USPSTF) or 45 years (ACS) with colonoscopy every 10 years.
  • Alternatives: fecal occult blood immunochemistry (FIT) annually, flexible sigmoidoscopy every 5 years + FIT every 3 years, or CT colonography every 5 years.
  • Colonoscopy allows detection and resection of adenomatous polyps (secondary prevention).
  • In a first-degree family history with CRC: start at age 40 or 10 years before the youngest case.

items.foco: Lynch syndrome and familial adenomatous polyposis
items.title: Hereditary syndromes
items.contentHtml:
Hereditary syndromes

  • Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): mutation in DNA repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2); causes <5% CRC; is characterized by microsatellite instability (MSI-H).
  • Amsterdam II criteria: ≥3 relatives with CRC or associated cancer (endometrium, ovary, etc.) in two generations, one <50 years old.
  • Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP): mutation in APC; >100 colorectal adenomas; CRC risk >90% at age 40 if prophylactic colectomy is not performed.
  • In FAP, screening with annual colonoscopy from age 10-12.

items.foco: Most frequent location: rectum and sigma
items.title: Anatomical location
items.contentHtml:
Anatomical location

  • The most common location is rectum and sigmoid (~55% of cases).
  • In Western countries, the majority of CRC are left (distal to the splenic flexure).
  • Right colon cancer (cecum, ascending colon) is more common in women and the elderly; It is usually exophytic and produces iron deficiency anemia.
  • Left cancer tends to be infiltrative/stenotic, manifesting with a change in intestinal habit and obstruction.

items.foco: Clinical presentation: rectal bleeding and habit change
items.title: Clinical presentation
items.contentHtml:
Clinical presentation

  • Rectal bleeding (hematochezia or rectal bleeding) is the most common symptom in rectal and sigma cancer.
  • Changes in bowel habits: constipation alternating with diarrhea, tenesmus, sensation of incomplete evacuation.
  • Colic-like abdominal pain, distension, and involuntary weight loss are signs of the disease. advanced.
  • Intestinal obstruction is more common in left tumors; perforation is a surgical emergency.

items.foco: Right colon cancer: iron deficiency anemia
items.title: Right colon cancer
items.contentHtml:
Right colon cancer

  • It typically presents with iron deficiency anemia due to chronic hidden bleeding (mixed, non-visible stools).
  • Right tumors are more exophytic and voluminous, with less tendency to obstruct early.
  • colonoscopy is the diagnostic method; If there is de novo iron deficiency anemia in >50 years, right CRC must be ruled out.
  • Histologically, they are usually mucinous or with microsatellite instability (MSI-H).

items.title: TNM staging
items.contentHtml:
TNM staging

  • TNM (AJCC 8th edition): Tis (intramucosal), T1 (submucosa), T2 (muscular propria), T3 (subserosa/pericolonic fat), T4a (visceral peritoneum), T4b (adjacent organs).
  • N0: no nodes; N1: 1-3 nodes; N2: ≥4 nodes.
  • M1a: metastasis to an organ; M1b: >1 organ; M1c: peritoneal metastases.
  • Nodal evaluation requires ≥12 nodes analyzed for adequate staging.

items.foco: CEA as a tumor marker and monitoring
items.title: CEA
items.contentHtml:
CEA

  • CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen): serum glycoprotein; It is not diagnostic, but useful in post-treatment follow-up.
  • Normal value <5 ng/mL; Preoperative elevation is associated with a worse prognosis.
  • It is measured every 3-6 months for 2-3 years, then every 6-12 months up to 5 years.
  • If there is persistent elevation of CEA postcolectomy, suspect recurrence; indicate colonoscopy and thoracic-abdomino-pelvic CT.

items.title: Surgical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Surgical treatment

  • colectomy with lymphadenectomy is the curative treatment for localized CRC (stages I-III).
  • Oncological resection: proximal and distal margins ≥5 cm; central vascular ligation; lymphadenectomy that includes regional nodes (≥12).
  • In rectal cancer, total mesorectal resection (TME) is standard to reduce local recurrence.
  • Laparoscopic approach is not inferior to open surgery in oncological terms.

items.title: Adjuvant chemotherapy
items.contentHtml:
Adjuvant chemotherapy

  • Indicated in stage III (positive nodes) and in high-risk stage II (T4, perforation, poor differentiation, <12 nodes).
  • Standard regimen: FOLFOX (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin) for 6 months; alternative CAPOX (capecitabine + oxaliplatin).
  • In patients >70 years of age, the benefit of oxaliplatin is limited; consider 5-FU/LV or capecitabine as monotherapy.
  • In MSI-H stage II tumors, adjuvant 5-FU is not recommended (no benefit, it could even be deleterious).

items.title: Liver metastases
items.contentHtml:
Liver metastases

  • The liver is the most common site of metastasis (portal route).
  • Liver resection with curative intent is possible if there are <3 nodules, liver disease limited to one lobe, and preserved liver function.
  • Perioperative chemotherapy: FOLFOX or FOLFIRI (irinotecan) ± biologics (bevacizumab, cetuximab if native RAS).
  • 5-year survival after resection of liver metastases reaches 40-50%.

items.title: Follow-up
items.contentHtml:
Follow-up

  • Colonoscopy one year after surgery (if complete preoperative colonoscopy was not performed, do it after 3-6 months).
  • If normal, repeat after 3 years, then every 5 years.
  • CEA every 3-6 months for 3 years, then every 6-12 months until 5 years.
  • CT thoraco-abdomino-pelvic annually for 3-5 years in patients at high risk of recurrence (high-risk stage III or II).
